title: My Take: Why AI Will Not Replace Software Developers — The Real Problem Everyone Misses
author: Akshay Dhillon
text:
Why AI Will Not Replace Software Developers — The Real Problem Everyone Misses
Every few months, someone claims that “software engineering is dead” or that AI will soon replace 90% of developers.
Yes, AI can now solve LeetCode, pass coding interviews, generate entire applications, and write more code than any human ever could.
But despite all this, real engineering productivity in large systems has barely improved, and in some cases, AI even makes developers slower.
A Microsoft-backed study of 4,867 developers across three companies found that AI helps most with small, self-contained coding tasks. Developers using AI completed tasks 26–73% faster, but the gains fell to under 10% for complex tasks requiring large-codebase context. AI users also produced 21% more pull requests, while junior developers relied on AI 15–19% more than seniors. When tasks required navigating multiple files, error rates rose 40%, and the productivity benefit almost disappeared. The study concludes that AI is powerful for localized work but loses effectiveness as codebase size and dependencies grow. (Cui et al., Microsoft Research, 2025)
I’ve spent time thinking about why, and the answer is surprisingly simple:
AI is limited not by coding ability, but by the Intent Gap.
This is the fundamental mismatch between:
What humans mean, what humans say, what AI understands and what AI actually builds
Let me explain why this matters.
1. Software Engineering ≠ Writing Code
If Software engineering was just typing instructions, AI would have replaced us already.
Software engineering is about:
- Understanding incomplete requirements People rarely know exactly what they want. You have to guess, clarify, and fill in the blanks.
Example: A client says “Make a login page.” But they don’t say if it should have Google login, work offline, or allow phone numbers. You must figure these out.
- Refining scope with stakeholders. Scope means what exactly needs to be built. You refine it together by asking many questions.
Example: A stakeholder wants “A fast search feature.” You ask what things should be searched, how fast is considered fast (1 second or 0.2 seconds), and whether it should support filters.
- Discovering hidden constraints. These are rules nobody told you, but they affect the system.
Example: You build a feature that lets users upload photos. In your tests, uploading takes only 2 seconds. But after launch, users start complaining, “Photos are taking 20–30 seconds to upload.” Then you find out something no one told you: Most users have slow internet or weak mobile data in their area. So even though your code is fast, the real world slows everything down.
- Handling ambiguity, Ambiguity means no one knows the answer, so you decide.
Example: The PM asks whether data should be stored in the cloud or locally. No one is sure, so you compare options, look at pros and cons, and make a decision.
- Understanding domain rules, every industry has rules that must be followed.
Example: You are building an online payment feature. You want users to transfer money instantly. Then you learn a domain rule: Banks do not allow instant transfers above ₹1,00,000 without extra verification (like OTP, PIN, or KYC checks). So even if your app can technically send the money, you must add these extra steps — because the finance domain requires it.
This rule completely affects how you design the feature.
- Making trade-offs, You can’t optimize everything, so you choose what to sacrifice.
Example: You want your app’s home screen to load instantly. To make it fast, you decide to preload some data in the background. This makes the app feel smoother, but it uses more battery and mobile data. So you improved speed, but you sacrificed battery and data usage. That’s a trade-off.
- Debugging unexpected behavior. Most bugs happen in ways you never predicted.
Example: You build a feature where users can save items for later. You design it like a wishlist. But after launch, customers complain that “saved items are disappearing.” You later discover they actually wanted the items to stay saved even if the product goes out of stock. You didn’t misunderstand the code — you misunderstood what “save for later” meant to them. This is not an AI or technical issue. It’s a requirement misunderstanding, and fixing it becomes part of debugging.
- Keeping systems coherent over years. Software becomes messy as it grows. Engineering means keeping it understandable and stable.
Example: A system starts small with 10 files and 5 endpoints. After 2 years it has 1,000 files, 70 endpoints, and contributions from 20 developers. Your job is to keep everything clean so nothing collapses.
Software engineering is the ongoing process of refining scope, and coding is simply the medium we use to specify that evolving scope.
And scope is never perfect the first time. You refine it continuously — daily, hourly!
This is exactly where AI struggles.
2. Requirements Are Always Incomplete — and AI Breaks on Incompleteness
In real companies, requirements are often unclear. They change halfway through, they miss important edge cases, and they depend on old system behaviors no one has documented.
Many expectations are never written anywhere — they live in people’s heads. AI cannot magically know these hidden details. So it fills the gaps using assumptions that might be correct in other projects, but completely wrong for your product.
This mismatch creates wrong features, weird bugs, and logic issues that take longer to debug than writing the code yourself.
3. Coding Is Easy — Understanding Is Hard
AI accelerates coding, but coding is the smallest part of engineering.
The real bottleneck is verification.
After AI produces code, humans still must:
- Understand what the AI assumed
- Check if it aligns with business rules
- Validate domain logic
- Check edge cases
- Test integration with existing systems
- Debug mismatches
- fix new errors introduced by those fixes
This verification costs more time than the “speedup” AI provides.
A 2025 study by METR showed that experienced developers took 19% longer on real tasks when using AI — even though they felt faster.
Reference: https://metr.org/blog/2025-07-10-early-2025-ai-experienced-os-dev-study
4. The More AI Writes, the Less You Understand — and Debugging Explodes
Here is the painful paradox:
When AI writes the code, you didn’t write it.
And if you didn’t write it, you don’t fully understand it.
When something breaks months later, you must:
- Reverse-engineer AI logic
- Understand assumptions it made
- Figure out why an edge case fails
- Diagnose a subtle misinterpretation of requirements
- This debugging workload grows exponentially with AI-generated code.
AI reduces typing, but increases cognitive load.
The net effect?
Small or no productivity improvement in real production systems.
5. CEOs See Demos — Not Reality
AI demos are clean:
“Build me an app.”
“Fix this bug.”
“Write this service.”
They don’t include:
- legacy systems
- half-written documentation
- ambiguous requirements
- business constraints
- compliance rules
- flaky tests
- dependencies across 10 teams
- hidden side effects
Engineering lives in this messy world. AI doesn’t.
That’s why CEOs think coding = engineering,
and why engineers know that coding is maybe 10% of the job.
6. So Will AI Replace Developers?
Not until AI can:
- Understand evolving human intent, and humans are not good at expressing what they want
- Resolve ambiguity
- Refine requirements dynamically
- Reason about domain-specific rules
- Know what isn’t said
- Avoid making assumptions
- Explain why it chose a certain approach
- Self-verify alignment with business logic
These are human problems, not computational problems.
Even perfect reasoning models can’t fix human vagueness,
because software is literally the process of eliminating vagueness.
7. My Conclusion
AI will replace a lot of coding, but coding is only a small part of software engineering.
Software engineering is about understanding what people actually want, and what the technology can realistically support at what cost.
It’s about refining messy requirements, aligning everyone on the same vision, making decisions with incomplete information, designing systems that won’t collapse later, debugging confusing behavior, negotiating trade-offs, and thinking deeply about how everything fits together. AI can write code, but it can’t replace all of that.
AI today is a powerful assistant, not a replacement.
The future isn’t AI instead of developers.
The future is developers who think, design, refine — and use AI as a tool to implement faster.
As long as humans remain imperfect at specifying intent (and we always will), software engineers remain essential.
